title: NLP Debugging Checklist
concept: A systematic triage checklist: verify tokenizer alignment -> print decoded batch -> overfit single batch -> sweep learning rates. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: NLP in Production.'

exercises.prompt: What is the very first thing you should check when an NLP model fails to learn?

options.text: Tokenizer alignment and data pipeline issues
The name of the output file

exercises.explain: Before tuning model parameters, you must ensure that your text data is correctly converted into numbers (tokens) and matches the expected labels.

cards.title: Stop Guessing, Start Debugging
cards.body: Have you ever trained an NLP model for hours only to realize it's outputting complete gibberish? Debugging language models can feel like searching for a needle in a haystack. Let's learn a simple, systematic checklist to stop bugs before they waste your time!

cards.title: NLP Debugging Checklist
cards.body: An NLP Debugging Checklist is a step-by-step strategy to find and fix bugs in language models. Instead of guessing why a model isn't learning, we systematically check tokenizer alignment, print decoded text batches, attempt to overfit a single batch, and sweep learning rates.

cards.title: The Bakery Test
cards.body: Think of debugging an NLP model like baking a complex cake. You don't just throw everything in the oven and hope for the best. First, you taste the raw batter (verify tokenizer), bake one tiny cupcake (overfit a single batch), and test different oven temperatures (sweep learning rates) before baking the whole wedding cake.

cards.title: Catching Bugs Early
cards.body: Before running a 10-hour training job, a developer prints the first batch of data to the console. They notice the words are decoded as random symbols like '[UNK] [UNK] hello'. Because they caught this tokenizer alignment issue early, they save hours of wasted GPU time!

cards.title: Myth: More Data Fixes Everything
cards.body: Many beginners think that if their model isn't learning, they need a bigger model or more data. In reality, the issue is often a silent bug, like mismatched tokenizer IDs or an incorrect learning rate. Always check your data pipeline first!

cards.title: Chaos vs. Checklist
cards.body: Compare a messy, unstructured approach to debugging with a structured, step-by-step checklist approach.

left.text: Changing learning rates, tweaking model layers, and restarting training without checking the data.

right.text: 1. Verify token alignment. 2. Print decoded batch. 3. Overfit one batch. 4. Sweep learning rates.

exercises.prompt: Why is 'print decoded batch' an essential step in the NLP debugging checklist?

options.text: It automatically speeds up the training process
It lets you verify that the tokens represent the actual text correctly
It increases the size of your dataset

exercises.explain: Printing a decoded batch of tokens back into readable text allows you to visually inspect if your inputs match your labels and aren't corrupted.

exercises.prompt: True or False: You should try to train on your entire dataset before testing if the model can overfit a single batch.
exercises.explain: Overfitting a single batch is a quick test to prove your model is capable of learning before you spend time training on the whole dataset.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing step of the debugging checklist:
exercises.sentence: To make sure your model code actually works, you should first try to [blank] a single batch of data.
exercises.explain: If a model cannot perfectly memorize (overfit) a single batch of data, there is a fundamental bug in the model architecture or data pipeline.

exercises.prompt: Which AI model is following the NLP debugging checklist correctly?

options.text: AI Model A: "The model is failing to learn. Let's change the entire network architecture immediately."
AI Model B: "Let's decode the token IDs back into text to make sure the words match their labels before training."
AI Model C: "Let's increase the training dataset to 1 million sentences to fix the loss error."

exercises.explain: AI model B successfully printed decoded text and verified the token alignment, which is the correct first step in debugging. AI model A and C skipped verification and jumped to full training or architecture changes.

exercises.prompt: You are building a custom text summarizer, but the loss is not decreasing. What is the correct order of debugging steps you should follow?

options.text: Immediately train on the entire dataset for 100 epochs
Assume the tokenizer works perfectly without checking the output
Verify token alignment, print a decoded batch, overfit one batch, and sweep learning rates
Change the model architecture randomly until it works

exercises.explain: Checking your data, overfitting a tiny sample, and tuning learning rates are the golden steps to NLP debugging success. Next up: NLP in Production.
